title: RAIDEN FIGHTERS REMIX COLLECTION

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies this as a compilation of the Raiden Fighters arcade shooting games, emphasizing pixel graphics, high-tempo action, scoring systems, and online rankings. That strongly indicates a classic arcade shoot-'em-up/bullet-hell style collection. However, that gameplay identity does not map cleanly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype in the provided list, and forcing it into an unrelated archetype would be inaccurate.

overall_note: The set is strongest when it stays with other arcade vertical shmups and retro shooter collections. The two modern Raiden releases and Psikyo collection fit especially well; Capcom Arcade Stadium is broader and less identity-matched, while DoDonPachi Resurrection is a strong genre neighbor but less aligned than the shooter compilations and Raiden titles.
anchor_summary: RAIDEN FIGHTERS REMIX COLLECTION is a retro arcade shoot-'em-up compilation centered on classic vertical-scrolling shmup runs, dense enemy patterns, score-chasing mastery, replayability, and modern collection features like rankings and version variants.
gap_notes: There is no true base-game/direct companion candidate; the best fits are same-franchise or same-format shmup releases.

title: Afterplace

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for top-down exploration-driven action-adventure, with several good discovery-first comps. Best fits emphasize open-ended roaming, secrets, light-to-moderate combat, and handcrafted world discovery; weaker fits skew more linear, more punishing, or more dungeon-gated than Afterplace’s freer wandering structure.
anchor_summary: Afterplace is a single-player top-down/open-world action-adventure with light action-RPG elements: freeform exploration, hidden paths and dungeons, simple hack-and-slash combat, NPC conversations and choices, light gear upgrading, and a low-handholding discovery-driven structure.
gap_notes: The rail trends slightly toward tighter, more challenge-forward action games than Afterplace’s more relaxed exploratory identity.
Several candidates capture top-down discovery and combat well, but fewer match Afterplace’s specific mix of open-world looseness, quirky NPC interaction, and choice-led narrative outcomes.
No candidate is a true near-identical match; the strongest comps are close neighbors rather than same-identity equivalents.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very strong fit on top-down exploration, hidden routes, minimal handholding, secrets, and light action-adventure structure. More puzzle/manual-decoding and tougher combat than Afterplace, but the discovery loop is highly aligned.

Shares top-down exploration, shortcuts, secrets, NPC interactions, varied weapons, and upgrade-driven progression. More systems-heavy, combat-technical, and pressure-driven due to its timer, but still a close gameplay neighbor.

Good match for retro-styled top-down roaming, branching paths, secrets, and action combat. It is notably harsher, faster, and lighter on dialogue/choice structure than Afterplace, making it a slightly less direct fit.

Overlaps on top-down melee combat, secrets, exploration, and light stat/ability progression. However, it is more structured, boss-driven, and punishment-focused than Afterplace’s freer open-world wandering.

title: Path of Mystery: A Brush with Death

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently frames the game around a non-combat, chapter-based mystery investigation: revisiting a decades-old murder, gathering clues, interviewing suspects, and uncovering the truth. Metacritic explicitly labels it as a Visual Novel, while the description emphasizes authored story progression, detective focus, and no combat-driven loop. The evidence for hidden-object gameplay is weaker because clue-finding is mentioned, but explicit hidden-object scenes or object-list puzzle structure are not. Overall the strongest fit is visual_novel with detective-mystery framing.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: most candidates are story-heavy Japanese mystery adventures with investigation and deduction loops. The main weakness is that some picks drift into more puzzle-system-heavy or branching/high-concept territory rather than the anchor’s more straightforward linear murder-investigation visual novel structure.
anchor_summary: Non-combat, authored mystery investigation game with chapter-based progression, clue gathering, suspect interviews, and puzzle/deduction gating in a visual-novel/adventure structure.
gap_notes: Best fits are detective visual novels or mystery adventures centered on interviewing suspects, collecting clues, and solving murders in an authored campaign.
Candidates with heavier branching, bespoke puzzle modes, or more gamified investigation systems are still relevant, but are somewhat looser than pure linear detective-VN comps.
No obvious completely off-genre pick is present, but the lower-ranked titles are more adjacent than near-identical.

title: Tales of Berseria Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

overall_note: The set is strong and includes the essential base game plus multiple close Tales-series neighbors that share the same party-based action-JRPG identity. Scarlet Nexus is a reasonable looser comp but sits clearly below the Tales titles because its combat and campaign structure are more action-forward and less like the classic Tales loop.
anchor_summary: Tales of Berseria Remastered is a story-led JRPG/action RPG built around a fixed party, real-time combo-driven Artes combat, character progression, overworld/town/dungeon travel, and a long-form authored campaign. Best matches should preserve the Tales-style party action-JRPG loop rather than just anime presentation or narrative tone.
gap_notes: The original Tales of Berseria is an obvious must-include base-game pairing for a remaster rail.
Other Tales entries fit better than non-series anime action RPGs because they preserve the same party combat grammar, progression structure, and campaign flow.
Scarlet Nexus is acceptable as an adjacent action-JRPG neighbor, but it is notably less similar in party control and overall structure than the Tales candidates.

title: Towerborne

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

overall_note: The set captures Towerborne's core identity as a 2D co-op brawler with RPG progression reasonably well. Dragon's Crown Pro is the clearest benchmark, Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong genre neighbor, and Young Souls/River City Girls 2 are valid but somewhat looser due to mode scale or stronger emphasis on story/open exploration over the shared-session loot-brawler loop.
anchor_summary: Towerborne is a side-scrolling co-op beat-'em-up/action RPG hybrid: combo-driven lane brawling, up-to-4-player co-op, weapon/build customization, hub-to-expedition structure, and repeatable progression through gear, crafting, and difficulty scaling.
gap_notes: Dragon's Crown Pro is the strongest fit and should anchor the rail.
Castle Crashers Remastered is a strong inclusion, but its progression is lighter and more arcade-leaning than Towerborne's.
Young Souls fits the side-scrolling action-RPG brawler space well, though its co-op scale and campaign emphasis make it a bit less direct than Dragon's Crown Pro or Castle Crashers Remastered.
River City Girls 2 is relevant, but its open-city progression and classic brawler focus make it a looser match for Towerborne's loot-forward co-op expedition loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match on side-scrolling co-op brawler combat, class/gear progression, repeated dungeon runs, and loot-driven action-RPG structure.

Modern 2D brawler/action-RPG with gear upgrades, hub returns, and co-op, but more story/campaign driven and less tuned to 4-player repeatable party brawling.
Strong co-op side-scrolling beat-'em-up with leveling, items, and expanded progression, but more open-ended urban brawler than loot-centric co-op action-RPG.

title: Utawarerumono: ZAN 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 3D hack-and-slash action RPG centered on fast-paced combat, a 20+ character roster, and 50+ story missions. The Steam detailed description gives the strongest identity signals: '3D HACK-AND-SLASH REBORN,' 'mix and match flashy combat techniques,' 'evolvable abilities,' and 'create an unstoppable team' all support a character-action core with party-management elements rather than a pure solo combo-action game. The presence of a primary story mode following Mask of Truth, a base system for interacting with companions and strengthening characters, and an online/single-player Dream Arena side mode supports a campaign-driven, mission-based structure with secondary JRPG-story-RPG traits. Evidence is strong enough to accept, but not strong enough to place it in a more specific action-RPG subtype from the allowed list.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core: the direct predecessor is essential, and the Fate/Persona/Warriors comps all map meaningfully to roster-based anime action RPG combat with story progression. The main weakness is that only the predecessor feels truly indispensable; the rest vary in how closely they match ZAN 2's specific mix of mission structure, party systems, and story-driven progression.
anchor_summary: Utawarerumono: ZAN 2 is a mission-based anime action RPG/hack-and-slash built around a large playable roster, party management, character growth, story adaptation, base interactions, and optional co-op side content.
gap_notes: Utawarerumono: ZAN is the clear must-include and best fit by a wide margin.
Fate/Extella Link is a strong gameplay neighbor thanks to roster-based flashy battlefield combat and mission structure, though its battlefield control focus is somewhat more musou-like.

Persona 5 Strikers fits well on party-based action RPG structure and story progression, but its encounter flow and dungeon/travel structure are less directly aligned than ZAN's more straightforward mission-based hack-and-slash format.

Fire Emblem Warriors: Three Hopes shares mission structure, base interactions, and squad management, but its stronger strategic layer and battlefield command emphasis make it a looser comp than the others.

title: Pogui

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Pogui as a side-scrolling platformer with hazardous levels, running/jumping/dashing, stamina-managed movement, and world-by-world stage progression. Metacritic explicitly calls it a 'side-scrolling precision platformer' in retro pixel art style, which strongly supports precision_platformer as the core identity. The game appears level-based across 5 themed worlds with handcrafted challenges and traversal-focused play. Enemy presence and 'defeat this nightmare' language indicate light action elements, making action_platformer a reasonable secondary archetype, but the dominant loop is precise movement and obstacle navigation rather than combat.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong because most picks target 2D challenge platformers with level-based progression and movement mastery. The best matches are the pure precision platformers; the weaker side of the set drifts toward broader action-platformers with more combat, exploration, or classic mascot-platformer structure than Pogui’s tightly hazard-driven identity.

title: Dice A Million

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. title: Stillborn Slayer

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong because every candidate shares the anchor's run-based action-roguelike backbone, but it mixes very close top-down combat roguelikes with looser neighbors whose perspective, structure, or meta-progression differ more substantially.

title: Wayblazer Dämmerung

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: every candidate is a story-forward, party-based JRPG with turn-based combat and campaign progression. Best fits capture the anchor’s classic overworld-to-dungeon flow and rebellion/political-conflict framing. The weaker end drifts toward broader traditional JRPG similarity or a more crafting-centric subidentity that does not match the anchor’s exact structure as closely.
anchor_summary: Wayblazer Dämmerung is a single-player, party-based turn-based JRPG built around world-map travel, explorable isometric dungeons, tactical turn planning, nonlinear campaign routing, branching story outcomes, relationship/trust progression, and a notable gather-materials-to-craft-and-supply-towns loop.
gap_notes: The set covers classic turn-based JRPG structure well, but most candidates are less specifically aligned with the anchor’s isometric dungeon flow and town trust/supply progression.

title: Ys X: Proud Nordics

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Ys X: Proud Nordics as the definitive edition of an action RPG adventure centered on fast-paced combat, exploration of a large island/map area, boss fights, and an authored story campaign. The copy emphasizes 'fast-paced combat,' 'Cross Action,' 'Mana Action,' new bosses, a high-difficulty dungeon, and broad map/exploration content, all of which support a single-player fantasy action-RPG identity. The current computed taxonomy pointing to traditional_fighter is contradicted by the actual descriptive text, which clearly frames a narrative-driven exploratory action RPG rather than a PvP or match-based fighter. The strongest fit in the available taxonomy is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, though the world structure reads more semi-open than fully open world.

overall_note: The rail is strong overall because it includes the base game first and then closely related Falcom action RPGs with similar real-time combat, exploration, dungeon/boss structure, and narrative progression. The lower half becomes somewhat broader with adjacent action-JRPG comps, but still remains defensible.
anchor_summary: Ys X: Proud Nordics is a definitive/enhanced edition of Ys X: Nordics: a single-player Japanese action RPG built around fast real-time combat, paired-character mechanics, exploration across large interconnected areas/islands, boss fights, dungeon content, and authored story progression. The current fighter taxonomy is clearly erroneous; the anchor plays like a story-led action RPG with traversal/exploration and progression systems, not a match-based PvP title.
gap_notes: Ys X: Nordics is the essential must-include and should lead the rail as the base game/original version.
Ys VIII and Ys IX are the strongest non-base-game comps because they share Falcom's specific action-RPG pacing, traversal/exploration emphasis, and boss/dungeon loop.
Tokyo Xanadu eX+ is a fair but looser comp: similar real-time Falcom action RPG structure, but more hub/dungeon segmented and less adventure-exploration driven than Ys X.
Tales of Arise is a recognizable adjacent action-JRPG, but its party combat flow and overall structure are less tightly aligned with Ys X than the Ys-series neighbors.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Original version of the anchor’s definitive edition; same core combat, exploration, progression, and story foundation with Proud Nordics adding expanded content.

Falcom action RPG with real-time combat, character switching, dungeon runs, bosses, and narrative progression, but it is more hub/social-segmented and less adventure-exploration oriented than Ys X.

title: PancitoMerge

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.

overall_note: The rail has one excellent, near-definitive comp, two understandable but looser falling-puzzle neighbors, and one clear mismatch caused by a bad title/entity selection.
anchor_summary: PancitoMerge is a casual single-player score-attack puzzle game built around dropping physics-driven pieces into a container, merging identical items into higher tiers, managing space to avoid topping out, and replaying for better scores/leaderboard runs. Its closest gameplay identity is the Suika-style drop-and-merge puzzle loop, not hidden-object play.
gap_notes: The strongest gameplay benchmark here is Suika Game; it should anchor the rail.

Puyo Puyo Champions and Tetris Effect: Connected share stack-management and arcade puzzle structure, but their matching/chaining rules and competitive emphasis make them looser neighbors than true like-for-like comps.
The candidate meant to represent 2048 is actually 8Bit Hero, an action co-op arcade game, so it should be removed as a misleading recommendation.
The set is missing other direct drop-and-merge / Suika-like puzzle comps, which would fit better than classic versus falling-block puzzlers.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Nearly identical core loop: drop items into a container, merge matching pieces into larger ones, prevent overflow, and chase high scores in short replayable runs.
Strong on falling-piece spatial management, topping-out pressure, and replayable score-focused puzzle flow, but line-clearing Tetris rules are materially different from merge-evolution play.
Shares fast falling-puzzle sessions and board-overflow loss states, but its chain-building and versus attack design are further from PancitoMerge’s cozy single-player merge-score loop.
This entity is 8Bit Hero, a co-op hack-and-slash action game, not 2048; its gameplay identity does not fit the anchor at all.
